items.span_text: Recent studies highlight the predominant use of success rate as the main metric for agent evaluation, which fails to capture the nuances of partial task completion by language model agents (Liu et al., 2023a; Li et al., 2023). This approach does not differentiate between near-complete tasks and minimal task execution, treating both as equivalent failures. Alternative metrics like reward scores are available but lack standardization (Chevalier-Boisvert et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2022). To mitigate this issue, we introduce a progress rate metric to accurately reflect LM agents’ goal attainment at various stages.
items.title: AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents

items.span_text: We employ progress rate along with the commonly used success rate metric, which computes the proportion of tasks completed within T interactions.
items.title: AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents

items.span_text: Progress Rate is more informative and discriminative than success rate. The success rate and progress rate across various tasks and categories are presented in Table 3. Regarding the overall performance, the progress rate serves as a more effective differentiator between models. For example, Llama2-13b and Mistral-7b exhibit similarly negligible success rates (2.1% and 3.9%, respectively), but their progress rates differ significantly: 18.9% for Llama2-13b and 24.6% for Mistral-7b. This disparity suggests that Mistral-7b generally outperforms Llama2-13b. For models with substantial differences in success rates, such as Text-Davinci-003 outperforming Llama2-70b by 11.7% in success rate, Text-Davinci-003 leads the progress rate by 13.4% as well, which indicates the consistency in performance disparity between significantly different models. Investigating the agent performance on specific tasks, progress rate is often able to differentiate models that have similar success rates – for instance, on the Embodied AI and Game categories, the success rates of most of the open-weight models are similarly low, while they are able to make meaningfully different progresses. Also, the success rate can be influenced by specific characteristics of agents, for example, an agent like CodeLlama-34b often fails to generate the action “finish” when performing tool-using tasks, leading to a higher progress rate and lower success rate compared to CodeLlama-13b. In contrast, progress rate is less susceptible to these agent-specific features as it reflects the overall ability of the agent at each step.
items.title: AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents

items.span_text: After human annotation, we manually verified our labeled subgoals through multiple verification stages to ensure its quality, as detailed in Appendix J. More importantly, we conduct a user study to justify our proposed progress rate metric, asking human annotators to assess the progress of model trajectories and then evaluating its correlation with our automatic progress rate metric. Specifically, we gather 60 model trajectories for each of the 8 tasks from three strong LLMs——GPT-4, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and Deepseek-67b. Each trajectory is assessed by four authors of the paper. The individual human rater is asked to select progress score from {0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%} given trajectories and task descriptions, without seeing the automatic score. Mean of four scores is taken as the final human score for every trajectory. We show the Pearson correlation between human progress score and the progress rate in Figure 3, and report Fleiss’kappa κ to reflect inter-annotator agreement. Results show that progress rate highly correlates with human assessment on the progress where the Pearson correlation exceeds 0.95 on all tasks, and substantial agreement is reached among the annotators.
items.title: AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents

items.span_text: To address these issues, we introduce AGENTBOARD, a benchmark designed for multi-turn LLM agents, complemented by an analytical evaluation board for detailed model assessment beyond final success rates. AGENTBOARD encompasses a diverse set of 9 unique tasks and 1013 exemplary environments, covering a range from embodied AI and game agents to web and tool agents. Each environment, whether newly created or adapted from pre-existing ones, is carefully crafted and authenticated by humans to ensure multi-round and partially observable characteristics in a unified manner. Notably, we have defined or manually annotated subgoals for each data sample, introducing a unified progress rate metric to track the agents’ detailed advancements. As we will demonstrate in §4.2, this metric uncovers significant progress made by models that would otherwise appear trivial due to negligible differences in success rates.
items.title: AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents

items.span_text: The origin implementation of babyai provides a reward score. Different from the original reward, our progress rate is more dense and the agent does not need to accomplish many steps before getting a increase in score. Here we compare the difference between our progress rate and the original reward score, as shown in Table 17. We can see from this case that our progress rate better measures intermediate progress for agents.
items.title: AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents

final_answer.markdown:
## Why AgentBoard argues final success rate is insufficient

In §2.2 (Fine-grained Progress Rate), the paper makes three concrete critiques of the dominant "success rate" metric:

1. **It conflates very different outcomes.** Success rate "fails to capture the nuances of partial task completion by language model agents" and "does not differentiate between near-complete tasks and minimal task execution, treating both as equivalent failures." [1]
2. **Existing alternatives are non-standardized.** "Alternative metrics like reward scores are available but lack standardization." [1]
3. **It hides progress on long-horizon tasks.** Because success rate is a terminal binary, an agent that almost finishes a multi-step task is reported the same as one that barely started.

## What the progress-rate metric adds

AgentBoard introduces a subgoal-anchored progress rate to "accurately reflect LM agents' goal attainment at various stages." [1] Specifically, the paper documents the following added properties:

- **Joint use with success rate, not as a replacement.** The paper employs progress rate "along with the commonly used success rate metric, which computes the proportion of tasks completed within T interactions." [2]
- **A unified, subgoal-based signal across diverse tasks.** AgentBoard "defined or manually annotated subgoals for each data sample, introducing a unified progress rate metric to track the agents' detailed advancements." [3]
- **Discriminative power among weak models.** In §4.2 the paper reports that "progress rate serves as a more effective differentiator between models. For example, Llama2-13b and Mistral-7b exhibit similarly negligible success rates (2.1% and 3.9%, respectively), but their progress rates differ significantly: 18.9% for Llama2-13b and 24.6% for Mistral-7b." The paper generalizes the point in §1: the metric "uncovers significant progress made by models that would otherwise appear trivial due to negligible differences in success rates." [4] [3]
- **Robustness to agent-specific failure modes.** "The success rate can be influenced by specific characteristics of agents, for example, an agent like CodeLlama-34b often fails to generate the action 'finish' when performing tool-using tasks, leading to a higher progress rate and lower success rate compared to CodeLlama-13b. In contrast, progress rate is less susceptible to these agent-specific features as it reflects the overall ability of the agent at each step." [4]
- **Denser signal than ad-hoc environment rewards.** In the BabyAI environment, "Different from the original reward, our progress rate is more dense and the agent does not need to accomplish many steps before getting a increase in score... our progress rate better measures intermediate progress for agents." [5]
- **Human-validated alignment.** A user study in which four annotators rated 60 model trajectories per task from GPT-4, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and Deepseek-67b found that "the Pearson correlation exceeds 0.95 on all tasks" between human progress scores and the automatic progress rate, with "substantial agreement" among annotators (Fleiss' κ). [6]

In short, the paper's argument is that success rate is a binary, terminal, environment-specific summary that hides partial progress and is brittle to formatting artifacts; the progress-rate metric supplies a unified, subgoal-anchored, step-by-step signal that is denser, more discriminative among weak models, and aligned with human judgment.

Sources
[1] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, 2.2 Fine-grained Progress Rate, p. 4
[2] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, 2.2 Fine-grained Progress Rate, p. 5
[3] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, 1 Introduction, p. 2
[4] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, 4.2 Main Results, p. 7
[5] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, L.4 BabyAI, p. 24
[6] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, 3.2 Annotation Verification and Metric Justification, p. 6

locations.preview: Human-dependent Annotation One limitation of AGENTBOARD is its reliance on human-annotated sub goals to measure the progress rate of LLM agents. The subjectivity of annotators calls for multiple verifications just to ensure the uniformity of the data. Also, as the complexity of tasks increases, the number of subgoals and the granularity required in annotation can grow significantly. This makes the process less scalable and more labor-intensive. arguments.markdown:
## Why AgentBoard argues final success rate is insufficient

In §2.2 (Fine-grained Progress Rate), the paper makes three concrete critiques of the dominant "success rate" metric:

1. **It conflates very different outcomes.** Success rate "fails to capture the nuances of partial task completion by language model agents" and "does not differentiate between near-complete tasks and minimal task execution, treating both as equivalent failures." [[ev_433180485b8873f1]]
2. **Existing alternatives are non-standardized.** "Alternative metrics like reward scores are available but lack standardization." [[ev_433180485b8873f1]]
3. **It hides progress on long-horizon tasks.** Because success rate is a terminal binary, an agent that almost finishes a multi-step task is reported the same as one that barely started.

## What the progress-rate metric adds

AgentBoard introduces a subgoal-anchored progress rate to "accurately reflect LM agents' goal attainment at various stages." [[ev_433180485b8873f1]] Specifically, the paper documents the following added properties:

- **Joint use with success rate, not as a replacement.** The paper employs progress rate "along with the commonly used success rate metric, which computes the proportion of tasks completed within T interactions." [[ev_ba868a1ccf1e2b44]]
- **A unified, subgoal-based signal across diverse tasks.** AgentBoard "defined or manually annotated subgoals for each data sample, introducing a unified progress rate metric to track the agents' detailed advancements." [[ev_74e419015b392d3c]]
- **Discriminative power among weak models.** In §4.2 the paper reports that "progress rate serves as a more effective differentiator between models. For example, Llama2-13b and Mistral-7b exhibit similarly negligible success rates (2.1% and 3.9%, respectively), but their progress rates differ significantly: 18.9% for Llama2-13b and 24.6% for Mistral-7b." The paper generalizes the point in §1: the metric "uncovers significant progress made by models that would otherwise appear trivial due to negligible differences in success rates." [[ev_06c508841246c0a8]] [[ev_74e419015b392d3c]]
- **Robustness to agent-specific failure modes.** "The success rate can be influenced by specific characteristics of agents, for example, an agent like CodeLlama-34b often fails to generate the action 'finish' when performing tool-using tasks, leading to a higher progress rate and lower success rate compared to CodeLlama-13b. In contrast, progress rate is less susceptible to these agent-specific features as it reflects the overall ability of the agent at each step." [[ev_06c508841246c0a8]]
- **Denser signal than ad-hoc environment rewards.** In the BabyAI environment, "Different from the original reward, our progress rate is more dense and the agent does not need to accomplish many steps before getting a increase in score... our progress rate better measures intermediate progress for agents." [[ev_756c64bd7c0ba0fe]]
- **Human-validated alignment.** A user study in which four annotators rated 60 model trajectories per task from GPT-4, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and Deepseek-67b found that "the Pearson correlation exceeds 0.95 on all tasks" between human progress scores and the automatic progress rate, with "substantial agreement" among annotators (Fleiss' κ). [[ev_9ab58fc10db4ee86]]

In short, the paper's argument is that success rate is a binary, terminal, environment-specific summary that hides partial progress and is brittle to formatting artifacts; the progress-rate metric supplies a unified, subgoal-anchored, step-by-step signal that is denser, more discriminative among weak models, and aligned with human judgment.

## Why AgentBoard argues final success rate is insufficient

In §2.2 (Fine-grained Progress Rate), the paper makes three concrete critiques of the dominant "success rate" metric:

1. **It conflates very different outcomes.** Success rate "fails to capture the nuances of partial task completion by language model agents" and "does not differentiate between near-complete tasks and minimal task execution, treating both as equivalent failures." [1]
2. **Existing alternatives are non-standardized.** "Alternative metrics like reward scores are available but lack standardization." [1]
3. **It hides progress on long-horizon tasks.** Because success rate is a terminal binary, an agent that almost finishes a multi-step task is reported the same as one that barely started.

## What the progress-rate metric adds

AgentBoard introduces a subgoal-anchored progress rate to "accurately reflect LM agents' goal attainment at various stages." [1] Specifically, the paper documents the following added properties:

- **Joint use with success rate, not as a replacement.** The paper employs progress rate "along with the commonly used success rate metric, which computes the proportion of tasks completed within T interactions." [2]
- **A unified, subgoal-based signal across diverse tasks.** AgentBoard "defined or manually annotated subgoals for each data sample, introducing a unified progress rate metric to track the agents' detailed advancements." [3]
- **Discriminative power among weak models.** In §4.2 the paper reports that "progress rate serves as a more effective differentiator between models. For example, Llama2-13b and Mistral-7b exhibit similarly negligible success rates (2.1% and 3.9%, respectively), but their progress rates differ significantly: 18.9% for Llama2-13b and 24.6% for Mistral-7b." The paper generalizes the point in §1: the metric "uncovers significant progress made by models that would otherwise appear trivial due to negligible differences in success rates." [4] [3]
- **Robustness to agent-specific failure modes.** "The success rate can be influenced by specific characteristics of agents, for example, an agent like CodeLlama-34b often fails to generate the action 'finish' when performing tool-using tasks, leading to a higher progress rate and lower success rate compared to CodeLlama-13b. In contrast, progress rate is less susceptible to these agent-specific features as it reflects the overall ability of the agent at each step." [4]
- **Denser signal than ad-hoc environment rewards.** In the BabyAI environment, "Different from the original reward, our progress rate is more dense and the agent does not need to accomplish many steps before getting a increase in score... our progress rate better measures intermediate progress for agents." [5]
- **Human-validated alignment.** A user study in which four annotators rated 60 model trajectories per task from GPT-4, GPT-3.5-Turbo, and Deepseek-67b found that "the Pearson correlation exceeds 0.95 on all tasks" between human progress scores and the automatic progress rate, with "substantial agreement" among annotators (Fleiss' κ). [6]

In short, the paper's argument is that success rate is a binary, terminal, environment-specific summary that hides partial progress and is brittle to formatting artifacts; the progress-rate metric supplies a unified, subgoal-anchored, step-by-step signal that is denser, more discriminative among weak models, and aligned with human judgment.

[1] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, 2.2 Fine-grained Progress Rate, p. 4
[2] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, 2.2 Fine-grained Progress Rate, p. 5

[6] AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents, 3.2 Annotation Verification and Metric Justification, p. 6

## Why AgentBoard argues final success rate is insufficient

In §2.2 (Fine-grained Progress Rate), the paper makes three concrete critiques of the dominant "success rate" metric:
